apartment-complaint.md:
## File a complaint {#file-complaint}

Tenants can file a complaint about unsafe conditions, lack of heat or hot water, pests (e.g., rodents, insects), mold, lead hazards, broken locks, or other housing code issues. File online through the city’s housing or code enforcement portal or by phone (e.g., 311 or the housing hotline). You may need to provide your address, landlord or management company information, and a clear description of the problem. Photos and dates can help. You can usually request that your name be kept confidential from the landlord, though the agency may need to disclose it in certain situations (e.g., hearings).

After you file, the city may inspect the property. If violations are found, the landlord may receive an order to correct them within a set time. Failure to comply can result in fines or other enforcement. Keep copies of your complaint number and any correspondence.

## Housing standards {#housing-standards}

Rental units must meet basic health and safety standards. These typically include:

- Heat and hot water — Adequate heat during cold months (often defined by minimum temperature) and hot water in working order.
- Security — Working locks on doors and windows; secure common areas.
- Sanitation — No pest infestations that the landlord has not addressed; working plumbing; adequate trash disposal.
- Structure and safety — No serious leaks, structural hazards, or exposed wiring. Smoke and carbon monoxide detectors as required by law.

If your landlord has not addressed violations after notice, the city may inspect and require repairs. In some places, you may have the right to withhold rent or pay into escrow under strict procedures—do not withhold rent without legal advice, as the rules vary and mistakes can affect your rights. Tenant organizations or legal aid can explain your options.

## Get help {#get-help}

Tenant support organizations can help you understand your rights, draft letters to your landlord, and navigate the complaint process. Legal aid may be available for low-income residents facing eviction, serious code violations, or retaliation. Contact your local legal aid office or tenant union; many offer free or low-cost advice. If you are facing eviction, respond to any court papers by the deadline and seek legal help immediately—rights and defenses vary by location and situation.

For emergency conditions (e.g., no heat in winter, gas leak, major water leak), report to 311 or the appropriate emergency line; the city may respond quickly and order immediate repairs.
building-permits.md:
## Apply for a permit

Apply for building permits online or in person at the building department. You will need to submit plans, site information, and applicable fees. Small projects (e.g., fences, sheds) may use a simplified process; larger or structural work typically requires detailed plans and review. Allow time for plan review and any revisions.

## Permit types

Common permit types include new construction, additions, renovations, electrical, plumbing, HVAC, roofing, and demolition. Some work may be exempt (e.g., minor repairs); the building department can confirm what requires a permit in your area.

## Inspections

Required inspections are listed on your permit. Schedule each inspection when the work is ready; failing or missing inspections can delay your project. Inspectors verify that work meets code before you proceed or close out the permit.

## Check permit status

Use the online permit portal or contact the building department to check the status of your application or permit, view inspection results, and see any open items or fees.
business-licenses.md:
## Apply for a license

Apply for a business license online or in person through the city or county licensing office. You will need to provide business name, address, type of activity, and owner or responsible party information. Some businesses also need state or professional licenses; the office can direct you to those requirements.

## Renew a license

Business licenses are typically renewed annually. You will receive a renewal notice with the due date and fee. Renew online or in person before the expiration date to avoid late fees or enforcement action. Update the office if your business name, address, or activity changes.

## License types

Common types include general business license, home occupation permit, food establishment license, and street vendor or mobile vendor permit. Requirements and fees depend on the type of business and location. The licensing office can confirm what applies to you.

## Frequently asked questions

:::dropdown{title="Do I need a license to work from home?"}
Many jurisdictions require a home occupation permit or similar for home-based businesses. Rules often limit signage, customers, and employees.
:::

:::dropdown{title="What if I operate in multiple locations?"}
You may need a license for each location or a separate type of permit; check with the licensing office.
:::

:::dropdown{title="When does my license expire?"}
Expiration is usually on the application or renewal notice. Set a reminder to renew before that date.
:::
child-care-assistance.md:
## Apply

Apply for child care assistance through your state or local child care subsidy program—often online, by mail, or at a designated office. You will need to provide household income, family size, work or school schedule, and the provider you plan to use (or need help finding one). Documentation such as pay stubs and proof of enrollment may be required.

## Eligibility

Eligibility is usually based on income, family size, and reason for care (e.g., working, in school, or in job training). There may be limits on the number of hours covered and the types of providers that can be used. Waiting lists are common; apply early and keep your information updated.

## Find approved providers

Subsidy programs maintain lists of approved or licensed providers. You can search by location, age of child, and hours. Some programs allow care by registered family members or in-home providers if they meet program rules.

## Frequently asked questions

:::dropdown{title="How much will I pay?"}
Families typically pay a copay based on income; the program pays the rest directly to the provider. Copay amounts vary by state and family size.
:::

:::dropdown{title="Can I use my current provider?"}
Only if the provider is approved or willing to become approved by the program. Ask your provider or the assistance office.
:::

:::dropdown{title="What if I’m on a waiting list?"}
Keep your application updated and respond to any notices. Some programs offer priority for certain situations (e.g., homelessness, foster care).
:::
